hoses: Supply line from hydrant to Engine 181 intake
nozzles: Deck gun with 1¾-inch smooth bore tip
sceneElements.elevation: Level parking lot operation, 0 PSI elevation correction

problems.title: Deck Gun Flow
problems.question: Engine 181 is operating a deck gun with a 1¾-inch smooth bore tip at 80 PSI nozzle pressure. Use the smooth-bore flow formula: GPM = 29.7 × diameter² × √NP. What is the approximate total deck gun flow?
formulaBreakdown: Smooth bore flow formula: GPM = 29.7 × d² × √NP.
problems.instructorExplanation: The deck gun is operating with a smooth bore tip. Using the smooth bore flow formula, the master stream flow rounds to 815 GPM.
problems.explainMistake: Do not use a handline flow value for a deck gun. A smooth bore master stream is calculated from tip diameter and nozzle pressure.

problems.title: Pump Discharge Pressure
problems.question: Engine 181 is operating a deck gun mounted directly on the apparatus with a 1¾-inch smooth bore tip flowing approximately 815 GPM at 80 PSI nozzle pressure. The stream is operating 30 ft above the pump; use elevation pressure = height × 0.5 PSI/ft. There is no hose friction loss and no appliance loss. What pump discharge pressure should be set?
formulaBreakdown: PP = FL + NP + elevation + appliance loss.
problems.instructorExplanation: A mounted deck gun may have no hose friction loss, but the pump still must cover nozzle pressure plus any stated elevation.
problems.explainMistake: Do not stop at nozzle pressure when the question gives an elevation add-on.

problems.title: Alternate Deck Gun Tip Flow
problems.question: Engine 181 switches to a 1½-inch smooth bore deck gun tip operating at 80 PSI nozzle pressure. Use the smooth-bore flow formula: GPM = 29.7 × diameter² × √NP. What is the approximate flow?
problems.instructorExplanation: This requires using the smooth-bore flow formula instead of repeating a nozzle pressure value.
problems.explainMistake: Do not enter the 80 PSI nozzle pressure as the answer. The question asks for flow in GPM.

problems.title: Hydrant Pressure Drop
problems.question: Engine 181 is supplying one deck gun flowing 815 GPM. Hydrant static pressure is 80 PSI and residual pressure is 65 PSI. Use the hydrant flow chart: pressure drop % = (static - residual) ÷ static × 100. Chart: 0-10% drop = 3 more equivalent lines, 10-15% = 2, 15-25% = 1, over 25% = 0. How many additional equivalent 815 GPM deck gun streams could likely be supplied?
formulaBreakdown: A 10–24% pressure drop indicates approximately 1 additional equivalent line/stream may be available.
problems.instructorExplanation: This asks for available additional capacity using the hydrant flow chart, not total GPM. Pressure drop % = (80 - 65) ÷ 80 × 100 = 18.75%, which falls in the 15-25% chart range and supports about one more equivalent stream.
problems.explainMistake: Do not answer with the 815 GPM deck gun flow. First calculate the pressure-drop percentage, then apply the hydrant flow chart.

problems.title: Additional Attack Line Flow
problems.question: The deck gun is flowing 815 GPM. If one additional 185 GPM attack line is placed in service, what is the new total flow requirement?
formulaBreakdown: New total flow = deck gun flow + attack line flow.
problems.instructorExplanation: The total fire flow demand is the sum of all flowing devices. Adding one 185 GPM attack line to the 815 GPM deck gun creates a total demand of 1000 GPM.
problems.explainMistake: Do not recalculate the deck gun flow when adding an attack line. Add the new attack-line flow to the existing deck gun flow.
